quiz_questions.question_text: What defines a main-sequence star?

options.text: It is collapsing from a cloud
It is a white dwarf
It has exhausted all its fuel
It is fusing hydrogen into helium in its core

quiz_questions.explanation: Main-sequence stars are in the longest phase of stellar evolution, steadily converting hydrogen to helium in the core.

quiz_questions.question_text: How long will the Sun remain on the main sequence?

options.text: About 100 billion years
About 10 billion years total
About 1 million years
Forever

quiz_questions.explanation: The Sun has been on the main sequence for ~4.6 billion years and will remain for another ~5 billion years.

quiz_questions.question_text: On the main sequence, what maintains a star's stability?

options.text: Hydrostatic equilibrium between gravity and radiation/gas pressure
Tidal interactions from nearby companion stars or gravitational perturbations from passing molecular cloud complexes in the neighborhood
The angular momentum of the star's spinning motion, which generates centrifugal forces that push material outward from the rotation axis
Organized electromagnetic field structures threading through the star's plasma interior, generated by dynamo processes in convective zones

quiz_questions.explanation: The balance between inward gravity and outward pressure from fusion keeps main-sequence stars stable.

quiz_questions.question_text: More massive main-sequence stars are:

options.text: The same as all other stars
Hotter, more luminous, and shorter-lived
Cooler, dimmer, and longer-lived
Only slightly different from low-mass stars

quiz_questions.explanation: High-mass stars have higher core temperatures, fuse hydrogen faster, and exhaust their fuel more quickly.

quiz_questions.question_text: An M-type main-sequence star (red dwarf) can live for:

options.text: Less than 1 billion years
A few million years
Trillions of years
About 10 billion years

quiz_questions.explanation: Red dwarfs are so efficient with fuel that they can shine for much longer than the current age of the universe.

quiz_questions.question_text: What percentage of stars in the Milky Way are red dwarfs?

options.text: About 70-80%
About 30%
About 5%
About 50%

quiz_questions.explanation: Red dwarfs (M-type) are by far the most common type of star, making up the vast majority of stellar populations.

quiz_questions.question_text: The mass range of main-sequence stars is approximately:

options.text: 0.08 to about 150-300 solar masses
0.001 to 10 solar masses
1 to 1,000 solar masses
Exactly 1 solar mass

quiz_questions.explanation: Below 0.08 M☉ hydrogen fusion can't sustain itself (brown dwarfs); the upper limit is roughly 150-300 M☉ due to radiation pressure.

quiz_questions.question_text: As a main-sequence star ages, its core gradually:

options.text: Gradually decreases in temperature as hydrogen fusion efficiency declines and the core loses thermal energy through neutrino emission
Progressively accumulates iron-group elements from successive nuclear burning stages until the core is entirely composed of inert iron
Maintains identical temperature, density, and chemical composition throughout the entire duration of the star's main-sequence lifetime phase
Accumulates helium and contracts slightly, causing the star to slowly brighten

quiz_questions.explanation: As hydrogen converts to helium, the core slowly contracts, heating up and increasing the fusion rate — making the star gradually brighter.

quiz_questions.question_text: Energy transport in a main-sequence star depends on:

options.text: Exclusively through radiative diffusion, where photons gradually random-walk outward through the stellar plasma over millions of years
Exclusively through convective bulk motion, where rising and falling parcels of plasma transport heat vertically through all stellar layers
Its mass — low-mass stars have convective envelopes, high-mass stars have convective cores
Exclusively through thermal conduction, where direct particle-to-particle collisions transfer kinetic energy through the stellar interior medium

quiz_questions.explanation: Low-mass stars have radiative cores and convective envelopes; high-mass stars have convective cores and radiative envelopes.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Sun's luminosity has increased since its formation by roughly:

options.text: About 1%
About 300%
About 30%
It hasn't changed at all

quiz_questions.explanation: The 'Faint Young Sun' was about 70% as luminous as today; it has gradually brightened over 4.6 billion years.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is hydrostatic equilibrium?

options.text: A condition of complete mechanical rest in which all forces acting on a system are precisely balanced, producing zero net acceleration anywhere
A calibration procedure used by astronomers to precisely orient and focus a telescope's optical elements for optimal image quality during observations
The balance between the inward pull of gravity and outward pressure in a star
An equilibrium state in a planetary hydrological system where rates of evaporation and precipitation are exactly matched over a given timeframe

quiz_questions.explanation: Hydrostatic equilibrium maintains the star's size: if gravity increases, pressure builds to push back, and vice versa.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the proton-proton chain?

options.text: A type of chemical bond
A chain of atoms in a crystal
A method of measuring star distance
The dominant nuclear fusion process in Sun-like and lower-mass main-sequence stars

quiz_questions.explanation: The pp chain converts four hydrogen nuclei into one helium-4 nucleus, releasing energy in multiple steps.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the CNO cycle?

options.text: Carbon-Nitrogen-Oxygen gas emissions
A type of stellar wind
A catalytic fusion cycle dominant in main-sequence stars more massive than ~1.3 solar masses
A cycle of chemical reactions in nebulae

quiz_questions.explanation: The CNO cycle uses carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen as catalysts to fuse hydrogen into helium at higher temperatures.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a red dwarf?

options.text: A massive luminous stellar object with high surface temperature and extensive hydrogen-burning regions generating enormous radiative energy output
A small, cool, low-luminosity main-sequence star of spectral type M or late K
An evolved low-mass star in its final evolutionary stages, shedding outer layers while its degenerate core contracts toward the white dwarf phase
A catastrophic stellar explosion triggered by core collapse or thermonuclear detonation that briefly outshines an entire galaxy in luminous output

quiz_questions.explanation: Red dwarfs are the most common and longest-lived stars, with masses between 0.08 and ~0.6 solar masses.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a blue main-sequence star?

options.text: A main-sequence star seen through a blue filter
A star that has turned blue from age
A hot, massive, luminous star of spectral type O or B on the main sequence
A cold star

quiz_questions.explanation: Blue main-sequence stars are the most massive and luminous but short-lived, burning through hydrogen rapidly.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the mass-luminosity relation?

options.text: A gravity measurement
A relation between mass and distance
The ratio of stellar mass to planetary mass
L ∝ M^3.5 to M^4 for main-sequence stars — more massive stars are much more luminous

quiz_questions.explanation: This power-law relation shows that a small increase in mass leads to a dramatic increase in luminosity.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the main-sequence lifetime?

options.text: The duration a star spends fusing hydrogen in its core
The time to form from a cloud
The age of the universe
The time to collapse into a white dwarf

quiz_questions.explanation: Main-sequence lifetime ∝ M/L ∝ M^(-2.5 to -3), so massive stars have short main-sequence lives.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is convective mixing?

options.text: The gravitational redistribution of stellar orbits within a dense star cluster, causing members to exchange kinetic energy through close encounters
Physical vibrations transmitted to a telescope's optical assembly from wind, seismic activity, or mechanical components during an observation session
The overlapping of photons from multiple unresolved stellar sources within a telescope's field of view, creating a composite observed spectrum
The process where convection transports material and energy within a star's interior

quiz_questions.explanation: Convective mixing brings fresh hydrogen to burning regions and carries processed material outward.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the Eddington luminosity limit?

options.text: The lowest possible energy output that a hydrogen-fusing stellar object can sustain while still maintaining stable nuclear reactions in its core
The total radiative power output of our Sun, measured at approximately 3.8 x 10^26 watts, used as a standard luminosity reference in stellar physics
An observational method employed by astronomers to determine the intrinsic brightness and energy output of distant stellar objects using calibrated instruments
The maximum luminosity at which radiation pressure balances gravity, preventing the star from blowing apart

quiz_questions.explanation: Above the Eddington limit, radiation pressure exceeds gravity, driving powerful mass loss from the star's surface.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is metallicity?

options.text: The abundance of elements heavier than helium in a star
How metallic a star looks
The magnetic field strength
The iron content of asteroids only

quiz_questions.explanation: Metallicity (denoted Z or [Fe/H]) affects a star's opacity, evolution, and planet-forming potential.

quiz_questions.question_text: A main-sequence star has 10 solar masses. Estimate its main-sequence lifetime relative to the Sun's.

options.text: Approximately one hundred times the Sun's main-sequence duration, giving the massive star a lifespan of roughly one trillion years total
Essentially identical to the Sun's ten-billion-year main-sequence lifetime, since mass has minimal effect on hydrogen fusion reaction rates
About 1/1,000th of the Sun's — roughly 10 million years
About ten times the Sun's main-sequence duration, because the additional mass provides proportionally more hydrogen fuel to sustain fusion

quiz_questions.explanation: Lifetime ∝ M/L ∝ M/M^3.5 = M^(-2.5). For 10M☉: (10)^(-2.5) ≈ 1/316, so roughly millions of years.

quiz_questions.question_text: A star on the lower main sequence is fully convective. What does this mean for its evolution?

options.text: The star's fully convective interior paradoxically prevents hydrogen nuclei from reaching the temperatures and pressures required for nuclear fusion
The vigorous convective mixing drives the star's surface temperature upward until it evolves into a hot, luminous blue giant phase on the H-R diagram
It can access all its hydrogen fuel, extending its main-sequence lifetime
The enhanced convective energy transport accelerates the star's nuclear burning rate, causing it to exhaust hydrogen fuel at a dramatically faster pace

quiz_questions.explanation: Full convection mixes the entire star, allowing all hydrogen to be cycled through the core for fusion.

quiz_questions.question_text: You observe that a main-sequence star's luminosity is 10,000 times the Sun's. What can you estimate about its mass?

options.text: About 2 solar masses
About 20-30 solar masses
About 100 solar masses
About 0.5 solar masses

quiz_questions.question_text: The Sun will eventually leave the main sequence. What triggers this departure?

options.text: Exhaustion of hydrogen in the core, leading to core contraction and shell hydrogen burning
A close gravitational encounter with Jupiter destabilizes the Sun's internal structure, triggering rapid changes in its nuclear burning processes
A supernova explosion from a neighboring star sends a powerful shockwave that disrupts the Sun's hydrostatic equilibrium and alters core conditions
The complete depletion of helium reserves in the Sun's interior forces the star to seek alternative nuclear fuel sources for continued energy production

quiz_questions.explanation: When core hydrogen is depleted, fusion shifts to a shell around the core, and the star expands off the main sequence.

quiz_questions.question_text: A red dwarf's surface shows strong absorption lines from molecules like TiO. Why?

options.text: It's very hot
It's next to a molecular cloud
It's cool enough (~3,000 K) for molecules to survive in its atmosphere
It has a polluted atmosphere

quiz_questions.explanation: At the low surface temperatures of red dwarfs, molecules like titanium oxide can form and produce distinctive absorption bands.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two stars have equal mass but different metallicities. How might they differ on the main sequence?

options.text: The lower-metallicity star is dimmer
The higher-metallicity star is slightly cooler and more opaque, affecting its structure
They would be identical
Metallicity doesn't affect main-sequence stars

quiz_questions.explanation: Higher metallicity increases opacity, which changes energy transport, making the star slightly larger and cooler.

quiz_questions.question_text: Alpha Centauri A is very similar to the Sun. If it has 1.1 solar masses, is it more or less luminous?

options.text: Less luminous than the Sun because the slightly higher mass increases internal opacity, which reduces the rate of energy escaping from the surface
Ten times the Sun's luminosity because each additional tenth of a solar mass multiplies the fusion rate by a factor of ten in the stellar core
Precisely equal to the Sun's luminosity because the small mass difference of 0.1 solar masses has negligible impact on stellar energy output rates
More luminous — about 1.5 times the Sun's luminosity due to the mass-luminosity relation

quiz_questions.explanation: L ∝ M^3.5: (1.1)^3.5 ≈ 1.4-1.5, so Alpha Centauri A is about 1.5 L☉.

quiz_questions.question_text: If the Sun's core suddenly became fully convective (mixing all hydrogen), how would its main-sequence lifetime change?

options.text: It would increase significantly because all hydrogen would be available for fusion
The main-sequence lifetime would shorten substantially because convective mixing would accelerate the hydrogen consumption rate beyond sustainable levels
There would be no measurable change because the Sun's luminosity and fusion rate are determined solely by core temperature, not by mixing processes
The sudden onset of full convection would trigger a catastrophic thermonuclear detonation that completely destroys the Sun in a violent explosion

quiz_questions.explanation: Currently only the core's hydrogen is used for fusion; full convection would supply vastly more fuel, extending the lifetime.

quiz_questions.question_text: An astronomer discovers a main-sequence star with 0.08 solar masses. What makes this special?

options.text: This object holds the record as the highest-mass star ever observed, far exceeding the theoretical upper mass limit for stellar stability
It's at the very bottom of the main sequence — barely massive enough for sustained hydrogen fusion
A star of this mass is in immediate danger of catastrophic core instability that will trigger a violent supernova explosion within a few years
This object is actually a compact stellar remnant that has already completed its nuclear burning evolution and expelled its outer atmospheric layers

quiz_questions.explanation: 0.08 M☉ is the hydrogen-burning limit; below this mass, the object would be a brown dwarf.

quiz_questions.question_text: A massive O-type star has strong stellar winds losing mass at 10⁻⁶ solar masses per year. How does this affect its evolution?

options.text: The powerful stellar winds actually bring in material from the surrounding interstellar medium, causing a net increase in the star's total mass over time
Mass loss reduces its total mass significantly, altering its evolutionary track and final fate
The rate of mass loss is far too small relative to the star's enormous total mass to produce any measurable change in its evolutionary trajectory
The stellar wind modifies only the surface chemical composition and spectral appearance without affecting the star's internal structure or final remnant

quiz_questions.explanation: Stellar wind mass loss in massive stars can remove substantial fractions of their mass, changing how they evolve and die.
